title: SIEM Alert-to-Incident Ratio Trending
dataSources: `index=notable`, disposition fields (`status`, `urgency`, `owner`), optional `index=itsi` if incidents sync externally
spl:
index=notable earliest=-90d
| eval confirmed=if(match(status,"Closed - True Positive"),1,0)
| eval triaged_incident=if(match(status,"(New|In Progress|Pending)") OR match(owner,"SOC|Incident"),1,0)
| bin _time span=1w
| stats count as alerts sum(confirmed) as true_positives sum(triaged_incident) as in_flight by _time
| eval tp_rate_pct=round(100*true_positives/nullif(alerts,0),2)
| timechart span=1w first(alerts) as weekly_alerts first(tp_rate_pct) as true_positive_rate_pct
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Alerts.Alerts by Alerts.severity, Alerts.signature, Alerts.app span=1w | sort - count
description: Comparing ES notables to analyst-confirmed incidents shows whether the SOC is drowning in noise or under-escalating real cases. A falling ratio over 90 days may mean tuning drift; a rising ratio can indicate improved correlation or staffing effects worth validating.
value: Comparing ES notables to analyst-confirmed incidents shows whether the SOC is drowning in noise or under-escalating real cases. A falling ratio over 90 days may mean tuning drift; a rising ratio can indicate improved correlation or staffing effects worth validating.
grandmaExplanation: Comparing ES notables to analyst-confirmed incidents shows whether the SOC is drowning in noise or -escalating real cases вАФ so we know when protection is off, out of date, or bypassed.
implementation: Align `status` values with your ES workflow (Closed - True Positive / False Positive). If you use formal incident tickets, join notables to `incident_id` from a lookup populated by SOAR or ITSM export. Review weekly with detection engineering to separate tuning opportunities from genuine threat volume changes. Exclude test rules via `rule_name` lookup.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
вАҐ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security.
вАҐ Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=notable`, disposition fields (`status`, `urgency`, `owner`), optional `index=itsi` if incidents sync externally.
вАҐ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 вАФ Configure data collection
Align `status` values with your ES workflow (Closed - True Positive / False Positive). If you use formal incident tickets, join notables to `incident_id` from a lookup populated by SOAR or ITSM export. Review weekly with detection engineering to separate tuning opportunities from genuine threat volume changes. Exclude test rules via `rule_name` lookup.

Step 2 вАФ Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=notable earliest=-90d
| eval confirmed=if(match(status,"Closed - True Positive"),1,0)
| eval triaged_incident=if(match(status,"(New|In Progress|Pending)") OR match(owner,"SOC|Incident"),1,0)
| bin _time span=1w
| stats count as alerts sum(confirmed) as true_positives sum(triaged_incident) as in_flight by _time
| eval tp_rate_pct=round(100*true_positives/nullif(alerts,0),2)
| timechart span=1w first(alerts) as weekly_alerts first(tp_rate_pct) as true_positive_rate_pct
```

Understanding this SPL

**SIEM Alert-to-Incident Ratio Trending** вАФ Comparing ES notables to analyst-confirmed incidents shows whether the SOC is drowning in noise or under-escalating real cases. A falling ratio over 90 days may mean tuning drift; a rising ratio can indicate improved correlation or staffing effects worth validating.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=notable`, disposition fields (`status`, `urgency`, `owner`), optional `index=itsi` if incidents sync externally. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedвАФrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: notable.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

вАҐ Scopes the data: index=notable, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
вАҐ `eval` defines or adjusts **confirmed** вАФ often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
вАҐ `eval` defines or adjusts **triaged_incident** вАФ often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
вАҐ Discretizes time or numeric ranges with `bin`/`bucket`.
вАҐ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by _time** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
вАҐ `eval` defines or adjusts **tp_rate_pct** вАФ often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
вАҐ `timechart` plots the metric over time using **span=1w** buckets вАФ ideal for trending and alerting on this use case.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Alerts.Alerts by Alerts.severity, Alerts.signature, Alerts.app span=1w | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**SIEM Alert-to-Incident Ratio Trending** вАФ Comparing ES notables to analyst-confirmed incidents shows whether the SOC is drowning in noise or under-escalating real cases. A falling ratio over 90 days may mean tuning drift; a rising ratio can indicate improved correlation or staffing effects worth validating.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=notable`, disposition fields (`status`, `urgency`, `owner`), optional `index=itsi` if incidents sync externally. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedвАФrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

вАҐ Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Alerts.Alerts` вАФ enable acceleration for that model.
вАҐ Orders rows with `sort` вАФ combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 вАФ Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 вАФ Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Dual-axis line chart (alerts vs true positives), column chart (weekly alert volume), single value (90-day TP rate trend).
visualization: Dual-axis line chart (alerts vs true positives), column chart (weekly alert volume), single value (90-day TP rate trend).
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern вАФ correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
